mnemonics: VIRTUAL DESTRUCTOR: 'Virtual Demolition' - Use a virtual destructor in the base class to ensure the derived class's destructor is also called when deleting through a base pointer, just like a demolition crew takes down the entire structure, not just the top floor.
keyPoints: A destructor is a special member function that is called automatically when an object is destroyed to release acquired resources.
Destructors are called in reverse order of construction: derived class destructor is called first, then base class destructor.
If a base class destructor is not declared as virtual, deleting a derived object through a base class pointer leads to undefined behavior and potential resource leaks.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to declare the base class destructor as virtual when there are virtual functions or when the class is meant to be inherited from.
Not implementing a destructor in a derived class when it acquires resources (e.g., dynamic memory, file handles), leading to resource leaks.
Attempting to call a destructor explicitly for an object with automatic storage duration, which results in undefined behavior as the destructor will be called again when the object goes out of scope.
